NotFound.description: Could not find the requested resource.

metadata.title: niwo | Software, IT & Cybersecurity
metadata.description: niwo systems is developing a focused software, IT, and cybersecurity consulting practice built around secure systems and incident-response readiness.

Home.heroParagraph: We help startups, small and medium-sized businesses, and individuals understand digital risks, better secure their systems, and adopt new technologies responsibly.
Home.identityStatement: NIWO is a founder-led software, IT, and cybersecurity consulting practice based in Bonn, Germany, working remotely with carefully scoped engagements.

incidentHelp.title: Have you been affected by a cyberattack or suspect a security incident?
incidentHelp.description: Message us directly and briefly describe what happened. We will get back to you as quickly as possible and provide free initial digital guidance: what matters now, which steps you should avoid, and how you can begin limiting the damage.

whoamiLink.title: Read the whoami page
whoamiLink.text: A short profile of my background, current focus, and how I think about practical security work.

Home.servicesDescription: Practical explanations of cybersecurity, secure development, incident response, and AI-assisted engineering — structured to show what matters, where the risks are, and how these ideas work in practice.

approach.title: An honest, practical approach to security
approach.description: I'm Nikita, a Cybersecurity student at IU International University with a background in electrical engineering and technical project coordination. I started niwo systems to combine hands-on learning with real-world practice. Most of what I know comes from building things, taking them apart in controlled lab environments, and figuring out why they fail. This site is where I document that work in a way developers, learners, and small teams can actually use.

scope.title: Focused scope
scope.text: Notes stay centered on the flows, trust boundaries, and actions that carry real application risk.

testing.title: Realistic testing
testing.text: Testing is grounded in how modern web applications are actually attacked, not just checklist coverage.

reporting.title: Clear reporting
reporting.text: Findings and notes are written to be understandable, defensible, and useful without turning into jargon.

remediation.title: Practical remediation
remediation.text: Each topic is meant to make fixes and tradeoffs easier to understand, not just add another checklist.

phishingLab.title: Learn how to spot email scammers
phishingLab.description: A hands-on inbox simulation. Review eight realistic emails and decide: scam or legitimate? Takes about five minutes — no account needed.

aiDevSecOps.title: AI in DevSecOps
aiDevSecOps.description: How AI can support secure software delivery — from planning and code review to CI/CD, infrastructure, and monitoring — without removing human judgment.

agenticEngineering.title: Agentic Engineering
agenticEngineering.description: How developers work with AI agents, tools, permissions, approval gates, and observable workflows while keeping control over high-impact actions.

incidentResponse.title: Incident Response
incidentResponse.description: A practical introduction to triage, containment, evidence preservation, recovery, communication, and learning after a suspected security incident.

common.sourceNote: A compact selection of primary sources, standards, and public technical guidance used to ground this article.

metadata.title: Security topics, clearly explained | niwo
metadata.description: Six connected guides to web application security, secure development, pentest preparation, AI in DevSecOps, agentic engineering, and incident response.

index.title: All Topics
index.intro: Six guides that build on each other - from web app security to incident response. Each gives you a practical mental model, is honest about the limits, and links the primary sources.

metadata.title: Web App Security, explained | niwo
metadata.description: A practical guide to trust boundaries, access control, sessions, data handling, dependencies, and the limits of security testing.

webAppSecurity.title: Web App Security
intro: Web application security means making sure an application does only what it should. That must still hold when someone sends unexpected or harmful input. The code matters, but so do its configuration and hidden assumptions.
This page follows a request through an application. It shows where trust changes, where risk builds up, and why common safeguards are often misunderstood.

coreIdea.title: What web application security actually protects
paragraphs: The goal is not to make software impossible to attack. It is to protect data, prevent unauthorized actions, and keep the service available. Security controls support one or more of these goals.

The key idea is the trust boundary. The user controls the browser, so the server cannot trust form fields, cookies, headers, URLs, or uploads. The same rule applies when data moves between an API and a supplier, an application and its database, or two services with different permissions.

Many vulnerabilities start when an application trusts data too early. It may treat input as a database command, accept a user's claim about what they may access, or place stored data into a page as executable code. OWASP groups these recurring failures into broad awareness categories.
Frameworks can provide safe defaults. But they do not know which customer owns a record or who may approve a payment. The application still has to make those decisions.

howItWorks.title: How a request moves through an application
howItWorks.intro: Follow one request from the browser to the response. Each stage makes a different security decision.

steps.title: Transport
steps.text: HTTPS protects data while it travels over the network. It does not make the application behind it secure.

steps.title: Entry point
steps.text: The server turns the request into paths, parameters, headers, and cookies. All of that data is untrusted.

steps.title: Authentication
steps.text: The application checks who sent the request. It usually validates credentials, a session, or a token. This proves identity, not permission.

steps.title: Session
steps.text: A session or token represents the user after login. If someone steals or predicts it, they may act as that user without knowing the password.

steps.title: Authorization
steps.text: The server must check whether this user may perform this action on this record. A hidden button is not an access control.

steps.title: Input handling and business logic
steps.text: Validation limits what the application accepts. Business logic decides what the data means. Bugs here can allow negative quantities, skipped payment steps, or access to another user's record.

steps.title: Data access and output
steps.text: Database queries must treat input as data, not code. Output encoding stops stored content from becoming script in a browser. Input validation and output encoding solve different problems.

whereUsed.title: Where risk tends to concentrate
whereUsed.intro: The same weak points appear across many applications. These are good places to start.

items.title: Authentication
items.text: Check passwords, account recovery, rate limits, and multi-factor authentication. Recovery needs strong checks because it can bypass the password.

items.title: Authorization
items.text: Look for missing checks on records and actions. The server must enforce the application's real ownership and permission rules on every request.

items.title: Sessions and tokens
items.text: Review cookie settings, token lifetimes, and what happens after logout or a password change. Long-lived tokens need a clear way to revoke them.

items.title: APIs
items.text: APIs expose application logic without the interface around it. Every endpoint needs a clear access rule, including internal endpoints.

items.title: Dependencies and configuration
items.text: Packages, base images, and infrastructure defaults are part of the application. Track them, update them, and remove what you no longer use.

items.title: Error handling and logging
items.text: Detailed errors can expose the system. Missing logs make incidents harder to understand. Log useful security events, but keep secrets out of the logs.

items.title: Business logic
items.text: Valid steps can become harmful when combined. Examples include reused discounts, repeated requests, and skipped checks. Scanners rarely understand these rules.

example.title: A realistic request-flow example
paragraphs: A logged-in user asks an invoicing app for invoice 8241 as a PDF. The connection is encrypted. The session is valid. The endpoint confirms that the user is logged in, then returns the file.
But it never checks who owns invoice 8241. Any logged-in user can change the number in the URL and read another customer's invoice.
TLS, password hashing, and session handling all worked. The data still leaked because one authorization check was missing.
The fix is to load the invoice through the authenticated account. A framework or scanner cannot decide that ownership rule for the team.

risks.title: Common misunderstandings
risks.intro: These claims sound plausible. Each one leaves out an important part of security.

items.title: “HTTPS makes the application secure”
items.text: TLS protects the connection. It does not fix injection, broken access control, or business-logic flaws.

items.title: “Authentication covers authorization”
items.text: Identity and permission are separate checks. A valid login does not grant access to every record or action.

items.title: “Input validation replaces output encoding”
items.text: Validation controls what enters the application. Encoding controls how output is interpreted. You need both.

items.title: “UI restrictions enforce server-side security”
items.text: Users can bypass disabled buttons, hidden fields, and browser checks by sending requests directly. The server must enforce the real rules.

items.title: “A clean scan means a secure application”
items.text: Scanners find known patterns. They do not fully understand ownership, business rules, or how several small flaws work together.

items.title: “The OWASP Top 10 is a complete checklist”
items.text: The OWASP Top 10 is an awareness document, not a complete test plan. The ASVS provides more detailed verification requirements.

humanResponsibility.title: What testing can and cannot prove
paragraphs: A security test covers one version, one configuration, and a limited amount of time. It can prove that a weakness exists. It cannot prove that no other weakness exists. Every meaningful change can alter the result.
Security therefore has to stay part of development. Standards such as the OWASP ASVS help teams decide what to check. The risk and rate of change help decide when to check again.
items: A finding proves a weakness; a clean report does not prove the absence of one
Technical severity and business impact are not the same
Test results become less useful as the application changes
Tools support judgement; they do not replace it
Only test systems you own or have explicit permission to test

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Mark every place where data moves from someone else's control into yours.
Check identity first, then check permission for each action and record.
Validate input and encode output. They solve different problems.
Treat dependencies and configuration as part of the application.
Use the OWASP Top 10 for awareness and the ASVS for detailed checks.
Treat every test as a snapshot, not a guarantee.

sources.title: OWASP Top 10:2025
OWASP Application Security Verification Standard 5.0
OWASP Web Security Testing Guide
OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
Web security documentation

metadata.title: Secure Development, explained | niwo
metadata.description: How to include security in planning, design, coding, testing, release, and day-to-day operation.

secureDevelopment.title: Secure Development
intro: Secure development means thinking about security throughout the life of the software. It starts with planning and continues through design, coding, release, and operation. A final scan is not enough.
The aim is simple: find problems while they are still easy to change, and keep learning once the software is in use.

coreIdea.title: Security is a feedback loop, not a finish line
paragraphs: Every release depends on choices. What may the software do? Which data may it handle? Who may take sensitive actions? How will the team spot and fix problems later? Secure development makes these choices visible.

NIST groups the work into four areas: prepare the organization, protect the software and its development environment, produce secure releases, and respond to vulnerabilities. These ideas can fit a small team or a large company. The controls will differ, but the questions are similar.

OWASP SAMM also treats security as ongoing work across governance, design, implementation, verification, and operations. A late test cannot repair a vague requirement. And a good design will not protect a system that nobody monitors or patches.
A better question than 'Did security approve this?' is: 'Which risk are we reducing, what evidence do we have, and who owns the decision?'

howItWorks.title: A practical secure-development lifecycle
howItWorks.intro: These stages overlap and repeat. A small change may take hours. A high-impact system may need formal reviews. The basic decisions stay the same.

steps.title: Plan
steps.text: Describe what the feature should do, which data it uses, who will use it, and how it could be abused. Turn the important risks into requirements you can test.

steps.title: Design
steps.text: Map data flows and trust boundaries. Think through likely failures. Choose safe defaults and limited permissions before the design becomes expensive to change.

steps.title: Implement
steps.text: Use maintained frameworks and proven controls for identity, access, input, secrets, and encryption. Keep changes small enough to review.

steps.title: Review
steps.text: Review the requirement, design, code, tests, and configuration together. Tools can flag patterns. A person still has to understand the business rules.

steps.title: Verify
steps.text: Test the security requirements. Add code analysis, dependency checks, secret scanning, and focused manual tests where they help. Passing a tool is not the goal.

steps.title: Build and release
steps.text: Protect source code, build systems, credentials, and release files. Know what went into a release and keep a tested rollback path.

steps.title: Operate
steps.text: Monitor useful security events without logging secrets. Maintain dependencies and give users a way to report problems. Responsibility does not end at release.

steps.title: Learn and improve
steps.text: Use vulnerabilities and incidents as feedback. Fix the defect, then improve the requirement, test, default, or process that allowed it.

whereUsed.title: Where explicit decisions matter most
whereUsed.intro: Some decisions need a clear owner. A framework or scanner cannot make them for the team.

items.title: Identity and access
items.text: Define who may do what and under which conditions. Login proves identity. The application must still check permission for each sensitive action and record.

items.title: Inputs and workflows
items.text: Validate untrusted data on the server. Test what happens when valid steps are repeated, skipped, or used in a different order.

items.title: Secrets and cryptography
items.text: Keep credentials out of code and logs. Limit access, support rotation, and use established cryptographic libraries.

items.title: Dependencies and builds
items.text: Know which components and build steps enter a release. Apply updates in time, test compatibility, and keep a rollback option.

items.title: Configuration and deployment
items.text: Use safe defaults, separate environments, and restrict production access. Check that deployment automation applies the intended settings.

items.title: Logging and response
items.text: Decide what to log, who receives alerts, and how long evidence is kept. Prepare a path to contain, patch, and explain a vulnerable release.

example.title: Example: adding a customer-data export
paragraphs: A small SaaS team adds a CSV export for customer records. Only current administrators may export data from their own tenant. Each export must be logged, large exports need limits, and temporary files must not remain on the server.

The team maps the browser, API, database, and download path. It considers stolen sessions, changed tenant IDs, spreadsheet formulas, repeated exports, and fields that should never leave the system. The query is limited to the authenticated tenant and only approved columns are exported.

The code uses the application's existing authorization helper and CSV library. Tests cover normal use, non-admin users, another tenant's ID, revoked roles, dangerous cell prefixes, limits, and logging.
After release, the team watches export volume and failures. If a new edge case appears, it fixes the code and adds a regression test.

risks.title: Common mistakes and real trade-offs
risks.intro: Security work can become a ritual that produces little useful evidence. The team should make trade-offs visible and reviewable.

items.title: Treating a scanner as the process
items.text: Automated checks find repeatable patterns. They cannot define the trust model, understand ownership, or judge how a workflow can be abused.

items.title: Moving everything left
items.text: Early feedback is useful. But monitoring, patching, vulnerability reports, and incident learning only happen once a system is running.

items.title: Copying a framework wholesale
items.text: SSDF, SAMM, and ASVS provide structure, not a universal checklist. Choose controls that match the system and its risks.

items.title: Letting severity scores make decisions
items.text: A score does not know which data the system holds or how a failure affects customers. Priority needs context and an owner.

items.title: Patching without change control
items.text: Delaying an update leaves known risk in place. Applying it blindly can break production. Test, roll out in stages, and keep a rollback path.

items.title: Assigning security to one specialist
items.text: Specialists add depth. But developers, product owners, operators, and leaders each own different security decisions.

humanResponsibility.title: Limits, ownership, and proportionality
paragraphs: No framework, toolchain, or certificate proves that software is secure. Evidence applies to a specific version, configuration, environment, and set of assumptions. It gets weaker as the system changes.
The process should match the risk. A small internal tool needs less ceremony than safety-critical software. It still needs clear ownership of access, data, changes, and response.
Automation should make good decisions easier to repeat. People still set requirements, approve exceptions, accept remaining risk, and respond when reality differs from the design.
items: Name an owner for each material security decision
Record assumptions and evidence, not only approvals
Make exceptions narrow, time-bound, and visible
Keep production feedback connected to development
Revisit controls when the system or its impact changes

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Build security into the development method you already use.
Turn important risks into clear requirements before coding starts.
Combine design review, tools, focused tests, and production feedback.
Protect the build path as carefully as the application code.
Match controls to the risk and record who owns each exception.
Use vulnerabilities and incidents to improve the next release.

sources.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1 - NIST SP 800-218
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Software Assurance Maturity Model
Application Security Verification Standard 5.0
Shifting the Balance of Cybersecurity Risk: Secure by Design Software
Software Security Code of Practice - Secure design and development
CON.8 Software Development
sources.organization: German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI)

metadata.title: Pentest Preparation, explained | niwo
metadata.description: How to prepare an authorized penetration test with a clear goal, scope, safety rules, useful evidence, and a plan for fixes.

pentestPreparation.title: Pentest Preparation
intro: A penetration test is an authorized attempt to get around a system's security controls. The tester may use methods that a real attacker would use. But the work has a defensive goal, a written scope, and clear safety limits.
Good preparation makes the result useful. The owner and tester need to agree on the question, authority, scope, rules, and next steps before testing starts.

coreIdea.title: A pentest answers a bounded question
paragraphs: A pentest cannot answer the broad question 'Is this system secure?' It can answer a narrower one. Can a normal user access another customer's data? Can a compromised account reach admin functions? Can an exposed service lead to sensitive data?
A vulnerability scan checks for known patterns across many systems. A pentest uses tools and human judgement to explore likely attack paths inside an agreed boundary.
That boundary is not just paperwork. It is what makes the test authorized. Anything outside the written rules stays outside the test, even when it is technically reachable.
The useful output is clear evidence, practical fixes, and lessons for future work. A dramatic finding is not enough if nobody knows how to handle or fix it.

howItWorks.title: How a penetration test is prepared and delivered
howItWorks.intro: The legal and operational boundaries must be as clear as the technical target. The steps below cover the path from the first question to the retest.

steps.title: Define the decision
steps.text: State why the test is being commissioned and what decision the result must support: a release, customer assurance, a material architecture change, or an independent check of internal vulnerability management.

steps.title: Confirm authority
steps.text: Identify the owners of every target and obtain explicit written authorization. Cloud, hosting, payment, identity, and other third-party services may have separate policies or approval requirements. Owning one application does not mean you may test every dependency.

steps.title: Build the scope
steps.text: List applications, APIs, hostnames, addresses, environments, roles, and important data flows. Record exclusions and shared infrastructure. A scope should describe assets and trust boundaries, not rely on a single homepage URL.

steps.title: Agree the rules
steps.text: Document dates, hours, permitted test types, rate limits, prohibited actions, stop conditions, source addresses, communication channels, and how any scope change is approved. Denial-of-service, social engineering, persistence, and destructive actions stay excluded unless expressly authorized and controlled. If genuine adversary activity is suspected, pause the affected test and follow the incident-response path.

steps.title: Prepare access and safety
steps.text: Provide representative test accounts and role descriptions, accurate documentation, safe test data, and a technical contact. Decide whether production or a representative environment is appropriate, check backups and monitoring, and prepare a rollback or containment path for unexpected impact.

steps.title: Protect evidence
steps.text: Agree how credentials, customer data, screenshots, request traces, exports, and the report will be minimized, encrypted, shared, retained, and destroyed. Test evidence is often sensitive enough to become a security problem of its own.

steps.title: Test with checkpoints
steps.text: The provider performs the agreed assessment and keeps the named contact informed. Critical findings and operational problems follow an immediate escalation path; discoveries outside scope are reported for a decision, not silently pursued.

steps.title: Report and debrief
steps.text: Expect an executive summary, exact scope and limitations, methodology, reproducible evidence for each validated finding, contextual risk, affected assets, and actionable remediation. Anything that could not be safely validated should be separated, labelled unverified, and accompanied by the reason and uncertainty. A debrief lets developers and risk owners challenge assumptions and ask for clarification.

steps.title: Remediate and retest
steps.text: Assign owners and deadlines, fix root causes, and regression-test related paths. A retest verifies the agreed findings against a later version; it does not repeat the whole engagement or prove that no other weaknesses exist.

whereUsed.title: Choose the assessment that matches the need
whereUsed.intro: Security services overlap, but they answer different questions. Clear names prevent a team from buying one kind of evidence and expecting another.

items.title: Vulnerability scanning
items.text: Automated, repeatable discovery of hosts, configuration signals, and known vulnerabilities. It is useful for broad and frequent coverage, but results need validation and usually do not show how weaknesses combine in the application's business context.

items.title: Penetration testing
items.text: A time-bound assessment combining tools and human analysis to test whether weaknesses can bypass controls or create meaningful impact within a named scope. It is deeper and more intrusive than routine scanning, but still only a sample.

items.title: Security review
items.text: A broader examination of requirements, architecture, configuration, controls, or operating practices against defined risks or standards. The term is not one fixed methodology, so the statement of work must say which artifacts and environments are actually reviewed.

items.title: Code review
items.text: Source-assisted analysis of how security decisions are implemented. It can reveal unsafe data flows, missing authorization, and logic flaws, but its conclusions are limited to the code and context supplied and do not prove the deployed configuration behaves the same way.

items.title: Red teaming
items.text: An objective-led exercise that emulates a relevant threat actor across people, process, and technology to assess prevention, detection, and response. It is not normally designed to enumerate every vulnerability in one application.

items.title: Incident response
items.text: Work performed because a harmful event has occurred or is suspected: confirm impact, contain it, preserve evidence, eradicate the cause, and recover safely. A scheduled pentest can test controls; it is not a substitute for responding to a live incident.

benefits.title: Questions to settle before the test
benefits.intro: A credible provider should help refine these answers and explain any trade-offs before the start date, not discover them halfway through the engagement.
items: What exact question will the test answer, and which risks are most important to us?
Which named tester will do the work, and what relevant experience, qualifications, or required accreditations can the provider demonstrate?
Which methodology and coverage will be used, and what will not be tested?
Which accounts, documentation, source code, architecture context, or previous scan results would improve the assessment?
Which actions are prohibited, rate-limited, or require a fresh approval?
Who is reachable during testing, and how quickly will a critical finding or service impact be escalated?
How will evidence and credentials be protected, retained, returned, and destroyed?
What report, debrief, remediation support, and retest are included in the agreed price and schedule?

example.title: Example: scoping a small SaaS application
paragraphs: A small team is preparing an invoicing platform for its first enterprise customer. It wants independent evidence about tenant isolation and administrative access before release. The objective is not 'test our company'; it is to assess whether an internet user or ordinary tenant member can reach another tenant's records or privileged functions in the release candidate.

The scope names the web application, public API, authentication flow, and administration portal in a staging environment that matches production configuration. The team supplies synthetic data and accounts for an administrator, a standard member, and a suspended member. The production payment provider, corporate email, employee accounts, denial-of-service, and social engineering are explicitly excluded; the team confirms that no shared third-party infrastructure may be tested without separate permission.

The rules set working hours, source addresses, a request-rate ceiling, emergency contacts, and an immediate notification path for cross-tenant access or service instability. The team verifies backups and logging, agrees that evidence will be encrypted and deleted after the retention period, and schedules a developer for short-notice questions.

The report identifies a cross-tenant authorization flaw with reproducible evidence and a server-side remediation. The team fixes the shared authorization helper, adds regression tests across all roles, and requests a retest. The retest confirms that this finding is no longer reproducible in the new build; it does not convert the original, bounded assessment into a guarantee about the whole platform.

risks.title: Common scoping and interpretation mistakes
risks.intro: Engagement quality can be lost before technical testing starts or after the report arrives. These mistakes reduce safety, coverage, or the chance that findings will be fixed.

items.title: Testing without complete authority
items.text: Permission from an application owner may not cover the hosting platform, shared network, supplier, customer data, or another legal entity's assets. Resolve ownership and provider policies before any activity.

items.title: Writing 'test everything' as the scope
items.text: An undefined scope hides assumptions about environments, APIs, roles, mobile clients, and third parties. It also gives neither side a reliable basis for time, safety, coverage, or price.

items.title: Buying a scanner report as a pentest
items.text: Automation can support broad, repeatable discovery, but a penetration test also needs contextual analysis, careful validation, and human exploration of relevant paths. Ask who performs that work and how it appears in the report.

items.title: Testing production without preparation
items.text: Testing production can provide evidence from the real operating context, but it can affect availability and real data. Decide deliberately, involve operators, minimize risky actions, and keep stop and recovery paths ready.

items.title: Treating the report as a certificate
items.text: A report describes what was examined and observed under stated constraints. A clean result may mean no issue was found in that sample; it does not mean no issue exists.

items.title: Fixing findings without retesting the cause
items.text: Changing the exact line shown in evidence can leave the same authorization or validation flaw elsewhere. Remediate the shared cause, add regression coverage, and define what the retest will verify.

humanResponsibility.title: Authorization, coordination, and ownership
paragraphs: The system owner remains responsible for the test and its risks. A provider can suggest the scope, rate findings, and explain fixes. It cannot expand its own authority or decide which risk the organization should accept. Every target and activity needs approval from someone who is allowed to give it. Use a signed scope or contract and seek legal advice where needed.

A pentest is a sample from a specific point in time. Its value depends on the scope, access, environment, time, method, and tester. A confirmed finding proves a weakness under those conditions. Missing findings do not prove that no other weakness exists.
Treat the report and evidence as sensitive data. Give fixes clear owners and priorities. A retest should name the findings, version, and date it covers.
items: Authorize every target and activity in writing
Name technical and risk-owner contacts
Record exclusions as explicit limitations
Escalate critical findings and service impact immediately
Protect and destroy evidence on an agreed schedule
Track remediation and retest scope separately

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Start with the decision the test needs to support.
Get written authorization and agree on scope and rules before testing.
Choose the type of assessment that fits the question.
Prepare accounts, contacts, safe data, monitoring, and evidence handling.
Require clear proof, limits, context, and practical fixes in the report.
Treat the result as a dated sample, fix root causes, and retest agreed findings.

sources.title: Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment - NIST SP 800-115
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Web Security Testing Guide
Guide to Penetration Testing 2022
Penetration testing guidance
A cyber buyer's guide to Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing: part 1
Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations - NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

metadata.title: AI in DevSecOps, explained | niwo
metadata.description: Where AI can help secure software delivery, where it can fail, and why people still own the decisions.

aiDevSecOps.title: AI in DevSecOps
intro: DevSecOps makes security part of everyday software work. It does not wait for one final check before release. AI can help sort findings, suggest tests, explain code, and point out patterns a team may have missed.
But AI is support, not authority. It does not understand a company's risk, customers, or legal duties. It can speed up useful work. It can also produce convincing mistakes just as quickly.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: Software delivery has several checkpoints: plan, code, build, test, deploy, and monitor. Security questions belong at each one. AI can compare, summarize, classify, and suggest. Normal controls still decide what may move forward.

This is more than asking a chatbot whether code looks secure. A useful process combines requirements, threat models, scan results, tests, build records, deployment rules, and production signals. AI may connect the evidence, but the evidence must remain visible.

howItWorks.title: How it works in practice
howItWorks.intro: There is no single AI-in-DevSecOps product or universal workflow. A careful implementation usually adds bounded assistance to an existing secure development process.

steps.title: Plan
steps.text: The team defines security requirements, likely misuse cases, sensitive data, and the actions that always require human approval. AI can help retrieve similar past decisions, but it should not invent the risk tolerance.

steps.title: Code
steps.text: A coding assistant may explain unfamiliar functions, draft tests, or suggest a safer API. The developer reviews the actual diff, checks assumptions, and remains responsible for what enters the repository.

steps.title: Verify
steps.text: Static analysis, dependency checks, secret scanning, and tests produce findings. AI can group duplicates or add context, but deterministic tools and reproducible tests remain the source of evidence.

steps.title: Build
steps.text: The build system records where code and dependencies came from and applies policy gates. An AI suggestion must not bypass signed artifacts, protected branches, or required reviews.

steps.title: Deploy
steps.text: Infrastructure changes are compared with an approved plan. Low-risk changes may be automated; access-control, production-data, and destructive changes should be narrowly permissioned and explicitly approved.

steps.title: Observe and learn
steps.text: Runtime signals, incidents, and false alarms feed back into tests and rules. AI can summarize a noisy event stream, while responders verify the evidence before changing systems or declaring an incident.

whereUsed.title: Where it is useful
whereUsed.intro: The strongest use cases have clear inputs, reviewable outputs, and a safe way to reject the suggestion.

items.title: Code and test review
items.text: Explaining a risky code path, proposing edge-case tests, or helping a reviewer trace data from an input to a sensitive operation.

items.title: Finding triage
items.text: Grouping related scanner results, adding repository context, and drafting a first explanation so specialists can spend more time on the findings that matter.

items.title: Infrastructure changes
items.text: Comparing configuration or infrastructure-as-code changes with policy, then presenting the difference for approval rather than silently applying it.

items.title: Monitoring support
items.text: Summarizing alerts and connecting them to recent deployments, while keeping raw logs, timelines, and source evidence available to responders.

benefits.title: What it can improve
benefits.intro: AI is most valuable when it helps people get useful security feedback earlier and apply existing knowledge more consistently.
items: Shorter feedback loops between a code change and a security question.
Less time spent sorting repetitive findings and searching for context.
More consistent draft documentation, tests, and review prompts.
Better access to security guidance for developers who are not specialists.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: AI can sound certain when it is wrong. Faster output is not stronger evidence. Automation can scale good and bad decisions.

items.title: Insecure or invented output
items.text: Generated code may contain subtle vulnerabilities, depend on nonexistent behavior, or solve the visible test while missing the real requirement.

items.title: Sensitive-data exposure
items.text: Source code, secrets, logs, and customer data can leak through prompts, tool integrations, retention settings, or an untrusted model supply chain.

items.title: Automation bias
items.text: People may accept a fluent recommendation too quickly or stop investigating after the system labels a finding as harmless.

items.title: Adversarial manipulation
items.text: Malicious code comments, issue text, logs, or dependencies can try to steer an AI-enabled tool. Inputs must be treated as untrusted, and tool permissions must stay narrow.

items.title: Changing behavior
items.text: Models, prompts, data, and connected tools change over time. Teams need repeatable evaluations and monitoring rather than assuming yesterday’s behavior will remain stable.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: People remain responsible for secure delivery. They decide which risk is acceptable, what evidence is enough, who may approve a release, and how to protect customers when something goes wrong.
Human review matters most when a change affects access, sensitive data, security findings, or production. A named owner must be able to explain the decision without saying, 'the AI said so.'
items: Define security requirements and risk tolerance
Approve high-impact or irreversible actions
Validate findings against primary evidence
Own incidents, disclosures, and corrective action

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: AI can support each delivery stage, but it needs an existing security process around it.
Repeatable checks and inspectable evidence still matter.
Keep automation limited, visible, reversible, and easy to reject.
Faster or more autonomous systems do not remove human responsibility.

sources.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1 — NIST SP 800-218
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Secure Software Development Practices for Generative AI — NIST SP 800-218A
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative AI Profile — NIST AI 600-1
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Guidelines for Secure AI System Development
Software Assurance Maturity Model

metadata.title: Agentic Engineering, explained | niwo
metadata.description: How AI agents use tools, permissions, memory, and feedback, and what keeps their actions under control.

agenticEngineering.title: Agentic Engineering
intro: An AI agent works through a task in several steps. It checks the current state, chooses an action, uses a tool, reads the result, and decides what to do next. A chatbot often gives one answer and stops.

Agentic Engineering is the work of making that loop useful and controlled. The field is new. Terms vary, capabilities change quickly, and no standard design makes an agent safe or reliable by default.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: A practical agent combines a model, instructions, tools, working state, and a loop. Its tools may read code, query a database, open a ticket, or propose a change. Memory can keep useful context. Policy limits what the agent can see and do.

A model can give a clear explanation and still be wrong. Important actions therefore need normal controls: identity, limited permissions, validated inputs, logs, budgets, stop conditions, and human approval when the impact is high.

howItWorks.title: How an agentic workflow works
howItWorks.intro: The exact loop differs between systems, but a controlled workflow usually contains the following stages.

steps.title: Scope the task
steps.text: A person or trusted system states the goal, boundaries, available data, success criteria, and actions that are forbidden or require approval.

steps.title: Form a plan
steps.text: The agent breaks the task into steps. The plan is provisional: new evidence may require revision, and a reviewer should be able to inspect the intended direction.

steps.title: Select a tool
steps.text: The system chooses from an allowlist of tools with structured parameters. It should not gain a general-purpose credential simply because one tool needs narrow access.

steps.title: Check permission
steps.text: Policy evaluates identity, task context, data sensitivity, action impact, and budget. A high-impact action pauses at an approval gate.

steps.title: Act and observe
steps.text: The tool performs the bounded operation and returns a result. The agent records what happened, validates the response, and uses it as new evidence rather than unquestioned truth.

steps.title: Stop or escalate
steps.text: The loop ends when success criteria are met, a limit is reached, uncertainty becomes too high, or a person must decide. A safe agent is designed to stop, not only to continue.

whereUsed.title: Where it is used
whereUsed.intro: Agentic systems are most credible when they operate in a defined environment with measurable outcomes and limited consequences.

items.title: Software engineering
items.text: Inspecting a repository, proposing a small change, running tests in a sandbox, and presenting the diff for human review.

items.title: Operations support
items.text: Collecting diagnostic information, correlating recent changes, and drafting a remediation plan without directly altering production.

items.title: Research workflows
items.text: Searching approved sources, extracting evidence, comparing claims, and producing a traceable draft that a researcher can verify.

items.title: Administrative processes
items.text: Preparing a ticket, routing a request, or assembling a report where each external action is logged and sensitive decisions remain with people.

benefits.title: What it can improve
benefits.intro: Agents can reduce the coordination cost of multi-step work, especially when information is spread across several tools.
items: Carry context across several steps instead of restarting with every tool.
Automate repetitive collection and preparation work around expert decisions.
Apply a documented workflow more consistently when the environment is well bounded.
Create useful action trails when tool calls, inputs, outputs, and approvals are recorded.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: Tools turn a wrong answer into a possible wrong action. Controls need regular testing because agent behavior and guidance continue to change.

items.title: Goal or instruction hijacking
items.text: Untrusted text in a document, website, issue, or tool result may try to redirect the agent away from the authorized task.

items.title: Excessive agency
items.text: A tool may expose more data or authority than the task requires. A small planning error can then have a large operational impact.

items.title: Cascading errors
items.text: One plausible but wrong observation can shape the next plan, tool choice, and action, making a multi-step failure harder to notice.

items.title: Memory poisoning
items.text: Incorrect or malicious information stored as memory can influence later tasks, users, or decisions long after the original input disappeared.

items.title: Poor observability
items.text: If prompts, tool calls, policy decisions, and state changes are not recorded, operators cannot reconstruct why an action occurred.

items.title: Runaway loops and cost
items.text: An agent can repeat unsuccessful actions, consume resources, or create load. Time, action, and spending budgets need hard limits.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: People choose the goal, tools, permissions, evaluation criteria, and approval rules. These are governance decisions. A prompt should not decide them.
Oversight also has to be realistic. Asking someone to approve hundreds of unclear actions is not useful control. Reviewers need enough context, time, and authority to say no.
items: Set task boundaries and least-privilege access
Approve consequential or irreversible actions
Review logs, evaluations, and failure patterns
Suspend the system and investigate incidents

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: An agent connects a model to a loop of observation, action, tools, and feedback.
Useful autonomy needs limited permissions, validation, logs, budgets, and stop conditions.
A strong demo does not prove reliable behavior in real work.
People remain responsible for goals, access, approvals, and incidents.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework 1.0
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative AI Profile — NIST AI 600-1
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Agentic AI — Threats and Mitigations
ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

metadata.title: Incident Response, explained | niwo
metadata.description: How teams detect, contain, investigate, recover from, and learn after a cybersecurity incident.

incidentResponse.title: Incident Response
intro: Incident Response starts when an organization suspects that an account, system, or data may be compromised. The work goes beyond removing malware. Teams limit harm, find out what happened, protect evidence, restore trusted operations, and communicate with the right people.

Speed matters, but so does care. Disconnecting a machine may stop an attacker. It may also interrupt a critical service or destroy useful evidence. Teams need tested plans, clear authority, and a record of key decisions.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: Incident response connects preparation, detection, response, and recovery. Preparation decides whether logs exist, backups work, contacts are known, and people can act under pressure. Detection and triage turn alerts into a working view of scope and impact.

The team then contains the risk, preserves evidence, removes the cause, and restores systems with care. Recovery is not finished until important services can be trusted again and lessons from the incident lead to real changes.

howItWorks.title: How the response unfolds
howItWorks.intro: Real incidents rarely follow a perfect straight line. Teams may repeat these stages as the scope changes, but the structure helps them avoid improvised and contradictory actions.

steps.title: Prepare
steps.text: Define roles, contacts, escalation thresholds, communication channels, evidence procedures, backups, and safe access before an incident occurs. Practice with realistic exercises.

steps.title: Detect and verify
steps.text: Collect alerts, reports, and system signals. Check whether the event is genuine, record initial facts, and avoid treating an unverified theory as the conclusion.

steps.title: Triage and scope
steps.text: Estimate which identities, devices, applications, data, and business processes may be affected. Prioritize by impact, urgency, and the attacker’s remaining access.

steps.title: Contain
steps.text: Limit further harm through actions such as isolating a host, disabling a credential, blocking a malicious connection, or separating a network segment. Choose actions proportionate to operational risk.

steps.title: Preserve and analyze
steps.text: Collect relevant logs, files, volatile data, and timelines in a way that protects integrity and provenance. Analysis tests competing explanations and refines the scope.

steps.title: Eradicate and recover
steps.text: Remove malicious persistence, close the exploited path, rotate affected secrets, rebuild or restore systems, verify backups, and monitor closely for recurrence.

steps.title: Communicate and learn
steps.text: Provide accurate updates through approved channels, meet legal or contractual duties, document decisions, and turn lessons into better controls, plans, and exercises.

whereUsed.title: Where it is used
whereUsed.intro: Incident response applies whenever digital trust may have been lost — from a single account to a major service disruption.

items.title: Account compromise
items.text: A phishing report or unusual login leads to session revocation, credential reset, mailbox review, and checks for further access.

items.title: Ransomware
items.text: Teams isolate affected systems, protect backups, determine the spread, coordinate business continuity, preserve evidence, and plan a verified restoration.

items.title: Cloud or API exposure
items.text: A leaked token or misconfiguration requires rapid credential control, access-log analysis, scope assessment, and validation that the exposed path is closed.

items.title: Application breach
items.text: Responders contain the vulnerable service, preserve application and infrastructure evidence, identify affected data, remediate the defect, and monitor the repaired system.

benefits.title: What good response improves
benefits.intro: A prepared response does not guarantee that an incident will be small, but it improves the organization’s ability to make defensible decisions under uncertainty.
items: Faster containment and less time for an attacker to cause additional harm.
Clearer coordination across technical, legal, leadership, communications, and external partners.
More reliable evidence for scoping, recovery, reporting, and later investigation.
Safer restoration and concrete lessons that reduce the chance of recurrence.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: Teams make response decisions with incomplete information. A playbook helps, but people still have to judge safety, evidence, business impact, legal duties, and harm to others.

items.title: Destroying evidence
items.text: Rebooting, wiping, or changing a system too quickly can remove volatile data and make the sequence of events harder to reconstruct.

items.title: Scoping too narrowly
items.text: Finding one compromised account or device does not prove the incident is contained. Shared credentials, lateral movement, and persistence may extend the scope.

items.title: Operational harm
items.text: Aggressive isolation can interrupt essential services. Containment choices must balance ongoing attacker access against safety and business impact.

items.title: Unreliable recovery
items.text: Restoring from an unverified backup or reconnecting before the root cause is addressed can return the organization to a compromised state.

items.title: Poor communication
items.text: Late, speculative, or inconsistent messages can harm affected people and undermine legal, contractual, and public responsibilities.

items.title: Human strain
items.text: Long incidents create fatigue and tunnel vision. Rotations, decision logs, handovers, and access to specialist support are operational safeguards.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: Response needs named decision-makers. An incident commander sets priorities and records decisions. Technical specialists investigate and contain. System owners explain operational effects. Legal, privacy, communications, and leadership teams handle duties beyond the technical work.

Tools can connect alerts, collect evidence, and run approved containment steps. People still decide whether the evidence is enough, whether an action is proportionate, who must be notified, and when operations can be trusted again.
items: Assign authority, roles, and escalation paths
Protect evidence integrity and affected people
Approve containment, disclosure, and recovery decisions
Document lessons and fund corrective action

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Incident response is coordinated risk work, not just technical cleanup.
Prepare roles, evidence, contacts, and communication before an alert arrives.
Contain the threat without causing avoidable harm to evidence or operations.
Recovery includes verification, communication, records, and lessons learned.

sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations — NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident and Vulnerability Response Playbooks
Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response — NIST SP 800-86
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

metadata.title: whoami — Nikita Wokurka | niwo
metadata.description: About Nikita Wokurka, the cybersecurity student in Bonn behind NIWO: real security work, documented honestly, from hands-on projects to carefully scoped reviews.

Whoami.title: whoami

paragraphs: Hi, I'm Nikita — a cybersecurity student with a technical background in electrical engineering and project work.
With niwo systems I'm building my own web and security project. Along the way, I want to explain cybersecurity topics in a way that makes them understandable, practical, and easy to follow.
This is where I document what I build, test, break, improve — and what I learn doing it.

focus.label: Area of interest
focus.value: Web application security, secure development, AI-assisted DevSecOps, and security hardening

mode.label: Mode
mode.value: Focused reviews, learning notes, research, and small projects

Whoami.signalText: In a digital world that keeps moving faster, I want to give people more orientation: explaining clearly where the risks lie, what security really means, and how to use systems more consciously.

phishing.title: Phishing Defense Lab
phishing.text: An interactive training tool that teaches you to spot phishing in five minutes — no account, no tracking. Built with Next.js; the detection scenarios use stratified difficulty sampling so easy rounds don't repeat.

owasp.title: OWASP Lab Detection Engine
owasp.text: A local-only lab pairing a deliberately vulnerable Flask app with a Python detection engine: twelve OWASP-oriented scenarios (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, broken access control, insecure deserialization, and more), each with a documented detection rule ID, structured JSONL telemetry, secure/insecure lab modes, and pytest coverage in CI.

website.title: This website
website.text: Niwo Systems is open source. If you want to know how I work, read the code — security headers, i18n, legal compliance and all.

foundations.title: Software Development
foundations.text: Alongside security work, I study at IU International University. My object-oriented programming course project — a CLI habit tracker with a layered architecture, salted PBKDF2 password hashing and full pytest coverage — was graded 100%.

now.heading: Right now
paragraphs: I'm studying cybersecurity at IU while building practical experience in web security, IT support, and secure development.

Right now I'm looking for working student, internship, or part-time roles in IT, security, or technical support — especially where I get to work with real systems, documentation, troubleshooting, and security topics.
I am preparing to take on small, clearly scoped web and security projects when the fit is right.

business.label: If you have a security question for your business

metadata.title: Imprint | niwo systems
metadata.description: Legal notice and provider information for niwo systems.

provider.title: 1. Provider Identification
provider.body: Information in accordance with Section 5 DDG
provider.name: Nikita Wokurka

nature.title: 2. Nature of the Website
nature.body: This website presents niwo systems, its consulting focus, and its technology and security work. Consulting engagements are agreed separately in writing.
nature.disclaimerText: Information on this website is not a binding offer. The scope, authorization, deliverables, timing, and fees of any engagement are defined in a separate agreement.

contact.title: 3. Contact
contact.body: For legal notices or formal correspondence, please contact legal@niwosystems.com. Postal correspondence reaches me at the address above.

liability.title: 4. Liability for Content
liability.body: Content is created with care; however, no warranty is given for completeness or accuracy. Links to external sites are provided for convenience, and responsibility for their content lies with the respective providers.

metadata.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy | niwo
metadata.description: How to responsibly report a vulnerability affecting the NIWO website.

Vdp.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy
Vdp.description: Responsible disclosure guidance for researchers reporting potential security issues.

overview.title: 1. Overview
overview.body: Security is a top priority for us at niwo. We appreciate the work of security researchers and the responsible disclosure of potential vulnerabilities.

scope.title: 2. Scope
scope.body: This policy applies to digital assets owned, operated, or maintained by niwo, including niwosystems.com and associated subdomains.

guidelines.title: 3. Guidelines
items: Do not exploit vulnerabilities beyond what is necessary to demonstrate them.
Do not access or modify user data without consent.
Do not disrupt or degrade our systems.
Provide sufficient detail so we can reproduce and validate the issue.

reporting.title: 4. Reporting
reporting.body: If you discover a vulnerability, please report it responsibly by emailing security@niwosystems.com.
reporting.calloutTitle: Include the following information where possible:
items: Description of the vulnerability
Steps to reproduce
Potential impact
Suggested remediation, if available

acknowledgement.title: 5. Acknowledgement
acknowledgement.body: We may publicly acknowledge valid reports that help us improve our security posture. Thank you for helping keep niwo and its users safe.

metadata.title: Privacy Policy | niwo
metadata.description: Privacy policy for this website covering email contact, AWS hosting and delivery, Proton Mail, retention, and tracking.

intro.title: 1. Introduction
intro.body: This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect personal data when you visit this website or contact us by email. We aim to handle information responsibly and transparently in line with applicable data protection law, including the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

controller.title: 2. Data Controller
controller.controllerValue: Nikita Wokurka, operating under the business designation niwo systems
controller.addressValue: Germany; external business address being finalized
controller.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or your personal data, you can contact us using the details above.

dataWeCollect.title: 3. Data We Collect
dataWeCollect.voluntaryIntro: When you contact us by email, we may process:
voluntaryItems: Your name or signature, if you include it
Your company name, phone number, or other contact details, if you include them
The content of your message and any attachments you send
dataWeCollect.voluntaryOutro: This information is used solely to review and respond to your inquiry.
dataWeCollect.automaticBody: When you visit the website, basic technical data may be processed automatically by hosting infrastructure, such as your IP address, request metadata, browser information, and access logs needed for secure delivery of the site.

purposeLegalBasis.title: 4. Purpose & Legal Basis
purposeLegalBasis.body: We process personal data to operate the website securely, respond to inquiries, and protect our systems against abuse or misuse. Depending on context, the legal basis is either our legitimate interests under Article 6(1)(f) GDPR or steps taken at your request prior to entering into a contract under Article 6(1)(b) GDPR.

storageRetention.title: 5. Data Storage & Retention
storageRetention.body: Unsuccessful business inquiries are normally deleted within six months after the last communication. Correspondence relating to an active client engagement is retained for the duration of the engagement and afterwards only where necessary to establish, exercise, or defend legal claims or comply with legal obligations. Contracts and invoices are archived separately under applicable statutory retention requirements rather than kept indefinitely in the ordinary mailbox. For privacy or security requests, we retain only the limited evidence needed to document and resolve the matter, and delete it when it is no longer reasonably required.

thirdParties.title: 6. Use of Third-Party Providers
thirdParties.hostingBody: Amazon Web Services (AWS) provides Amplify Hosting, CloudFront content delivery, CloudWatch operational logging, and S3 media storage. AWS may process IP addresses, requested URLs, timestamps, user-agent information, request headers, and delivery or security metadata to provide and protect the website. The Amplify deployment and media-storage origin are located in Frankfurt, Germany (eu-central-1), while CloudFront uses a global edge network. Amplify compute logs are retained for 90 days. Amplify Hosting access logs are retained by AWS until the Amplify app is deleted. Separate access logging for the media CloudFront distribution is disabled.
thirdParties.emailBody: Business email is provided through Proton Mail by Proton AG in Switzerland. When you contact info@niwosystems.com, legal@niwosystems.com, or security@niwosystems.com, Proton may process sender and recipient addresses, message metadata, message content, signatures, and attachments to transmit and store the correspondence. Proton states that Proton Mail data is stored in encrypted form on servers in Switzerland, Germany, or Norway and that encrypted offline backups may be retained for up to 30 days.
thirdParties.fontsBody: The website uses locally hosted fonts. No font requests are sent to third-party font providers when loading the site.

sharing.title: 7. Data Sharing
sharing.body: We do not sell personal data. Data is only shared where necessary to operate the website, comply with legal obligations, or protect our rights and systems.

rights.title: 8. Your GDPR Rights
rights.intro: Under the GDPR, you may have the right to:
items: Request access to your personal data
Request correction or deletion of your data
Request restriction of processing
Object to processing in certain circumstances
Lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority
rights.outro: To exercise these rights, contact legal@niwosystems.com.

security.title: 9. Data Security
security.body: We take reasonable technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access, loss, misuse, or disclosure. However, no internet transmission or storage system can be guaranteed to be completely secure.

cookies.title: 10. Cookies & Tracking
cookies.body: This website currently does not set a language-preference cookie and does not use non-essential tracking cookies, analytics beacons, advertising pixels, or session-replay tools. If this changes in the future, this policy and any required consent controls will be updated before the new processing is enabled.

changes.title: 11. Changes to This Policy
changes.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time to reflect legal, technical, or operational changes. The latest version will always be published on this page.

contact.title: 12. Contact
contact.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or about how your personal data is handled, contact legal@niwosystems.com.
